title: Cleaning and disinfecting a coop after disease
subtitle: Breaking the disease cycle before the next birds go in.
excerpt: Restocking a coop that held a diseased flock without a thorough clean is how disease cycles continue. Red mite eggs persist for years in timber cracks; coccidiosis oocysts survive cleaning with water alone; Marek's disease virus lingers in dust. This tutorial covers the disinfection sequence that actually breaks the cycle.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, 2018. APHA biosecurity guidance for backyard poultry keepers (gov.uk). University of Wisconsin Extension, Poultry Sanitation bulletin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A product approved for use against poultry pathogens. Household bleach is not effective against all pathogens (notably coccidiosis oocysts). Veterinary disinfectants approved under the Poultry Disinfectants Approval Scheme are listed on the DEFRA website.

Dermanyssus gallinae eggs laid in cracks and joints in timber coop structures. The eggs are resistant to many disinfectants and can remain dormant for months before hatching. Complete removal of all organic material and cracks is the only reliable control.

The environmentally resistant egg stage of Eimeria, the coccidiosis parasite. Survives routine water cleaning and many disinfectants; only steam cleaning and specialist products reliably inactivate oocysts in the environment.

content.text: A coop that has held a diseased or parasitised flock needs more than a sweep and a rinse before new birds go in. The pathogens that matter, especially red mite, coccidiosis oocysts, and Marek's disease virus, persist in litter, timber joints, and dust for months or years. The sequence below is the minimum disinfection protocol that breaks the cycle.

Remove all birds. Strip out all removable fittings: perches, nesting boxes, feeders, drinkers, dust-bath boxes. These are cleaned separately. Bag all litter and take it directly to a muck heap or sealed bin; do not leave it in the run where the next batch of birds will walk in it.

With all litter removed, sweep every surface: walls, ceiling, floor, and all joints. Scrape any dried droppings from perches and crevices with a stiff scraper. Pay particular attention to corners and frame joints where red mite hide. This dry step matters because disinfectants penetrate organic material poorly; leaving dried manure on a surface neutralises most of the product applied over it.

Pressure wash all surfaces with plain water. This removes the bulk of the remaining organic load and drives water into the cracks where red mite eggs lie. Allow the structure to dry for at least 4 hours before applying disinfectant; applying disinfectant to a wet surface dilutes it and reduces effectiveness.

approved for poultry. Mix at the terminal dose (higher than the routine dose) and leave for the full contact time before rinsing. If the flock had coccidiosis, ask your vet about a specialist product;

was confirmed or suspected, apply a specific red mite product to all perch sockets, joints, and crevices while the structure is still wet. Products containing diatomaceous earth or licensed acaricides penetrate cracks that general disinfectants miss. Leave the coop empty and sealed for at least a week after treatment before introducing new birds.

Allow the structure to dry completely and air for at least 48 hours after the final treatment before putting birds in. Apply fresh, dry litter to a depth of at least 8 cm. Do not restock with birds of unknown disease status from a market flock immediately after a disease event; source from a clean, tested source or allow a minimum empty period of 4 weeks.
